description: Check whether vulnerability assessment and penetration testing practices cover scope, attack surface, web, API, cloud, mobile, reporting, and retesting.

questions.question: Is the VAPT scope formally approved with in-scope assets, exclusions, test windows, and rules of engagement?
questions.description: The scope should cover IPs, domains, applications, APIs, cloud accounts, mobile apps, and business constraints.
questions.recommendation: Create a signed rules-of-engagement document with scope, methods, contacts, test windows, and stop conditions.

questions.question: Has the external and internal attack surface been discovered and reconciled with the asset inventory?
questions.description: Include domains, subdomains, exposed services, cloud assets, third-party hosted systems, and shadow IT.
questions.recommendation: Run attack surface discovery and reconcile findings with CMDB, cloud inventory, and ownership data.

questions.question: Are test accounts, roles, data sets, and environment constraints prepared before testing starts?
questions.description: Good preparation enables authenticated testing without disrupting production or exposing real data unnecessarily.
questions.recommendation: Prepare role-based accounts, sample data, whitelisted source IPs, and escalation contacts before the assessment.

questions.question: Does the methodology align to recognized testing guidance such as OWASP WSTG, OWASP API Security, or PTES?
questions.description: The methodology should define manual validation, exploit limits, evidence capture, and reporting expectations.
questions.recommendation: Adopt a documented methodology mapped to OWASP WSTG/API risks and tailor it to the target technology stack.

questions.question: Are both authenticated and unauthenticated vulnerability scans performed where technically feasible?
questions.description: Authenticated scans usually find missing patches, insecure configurations, and local weaknesses missed from outside.
questions.recommendation: Enable credentialed scans for servers, endpoints, cloud workloads, and applications where safe and approved.

questions.question: Are vulnerabilities rated with business context, exploitability, asset criticality, and CVSS or equivalent scoring?
questions.description: Risk rating should avoid raw scanner severity when compensating controls or business exposure change priority.
questions.recommendation: Define a risk rating model combining CVSS, exposure, exploitability, data sensitivity, and business criticality.

questions.question: Are network services, firewall exposure, segmentation, and administrative interfaces tested?
questions.description: Assess unnecessary services, weak protocols, exposed admin panels, lateral movement paths, and segmentation boundaries.
questions.recommendation: Review exposed services and segmentation rules, then remove unused protocols and restrict administrative access.

questions.question: Are patch levels, insecure configurations, default credentials, and weak TLS settings checked?
questions.description: Configuration weaknesses are often more exploitable than missing patches and should be verified manually.
questions.recommendation: Baseline systems against secure configuration standards and remediate weak TLS, default credentials, and missing patches.

questions.question: Are web applications tested for OWASP Top 10 risks and business logic abuse cases?
questions.description: Include injection, XSS, SSRF, file upload, security misconfiguration, access control, and workflow bypass.
questions.recommendation: Run manual web application testing mapped to OWASP risks and include business logic scenarios from product owners.

questions.question: Are authentication controls tested for MFA enforcement, lockout, password reset, enumeration, and session issuance?
questions.description: Authentication testing should include both user-facing flows and alternative channels such as APIs or admin panels.
questions.recommendation: Strengthen authentication by enforcing MFA, rate limits, secure reset flows, and consistent controls across channels.

questions.question: Are authorization controls tested for privilege escalation, IDOR, tenant isolation, and role bypass?
questions.description: Use multiple roles and tenants to validate object-level, function-level, and workflow authorization.
questions.recommendation: Add authorization test cases for every sensitive object, function, tenant boundary, and administrative action.

questions.question: Are session management controls tested for cookie attributes, timeout, logout, fixation, and hijacking resistance?
questions.description: Assess Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, session rotation, idle timeout, absolute timeout, and invalidation after logout.
questions.recommendation: Harden session settings and verify token rotation, timeout, logout invalidation, and secure cookie attributes.

questions.question: Are APIs tested for broken object authorization, rate limiting, schema validation, mass assignment, and token handling?
questions.description: Include REST, GraphQL, internal APIs, partner APIs, and mobile backends where applicable.
questions.recommendation: Build API security tests for authorization, throttling, schema enforcement, sensitive data exposure, and token validation.

questions.question: Are cloud IAM, storage exposure, network security groups, logging, secrets, and managed services reviewed?
questions.description: Cloud testing should validate identity privileges, public access, encryption, monitoring, and insecure defaults.
questions.recommendation: Run cloud configuration review and remediate excessive IAM rights, public storage, missing logging, and exposed secrets.

questions.question: Are mobile applications tested for insecure storage, transport security, reverse engineering, and API abuse?
questions.description: Apply this where Android or iOS applications are in scope, including mobile-specific backend behavior.
questions.recommendation: Perform mobile testing with static analysis, dynamic testing, transport checks, and backend authorization validation.

questions.question: Are exploitable findings manually validated while avoiding unsafe impact to production systems?
questions.description: Validation should prove risk with controlled evidence and respect approved stop conditions.
questions.recommendation: Define exploit validation limits and require manual proof for high-risk findings without damaging systems or data.

questions.question: Does the final report include executive summary, technical evidence, risk ratings, affected assets, and remediation guidance?
questions.description: Reports should support leadership decisions and engineering fixes without requiring separate interpretation.
questions.recommendation: Standardize report templates with business impact, proof, reproducible steps, affected assets, and prioritized remediation.

questions.question: Is remediation tracked and retested to confirm vulnerabilities are closed?
questions.description: Retesting should verify fixes, update residual risk, and produce closure evidence for audits or customers.
questions.recommendation: Track findings through owners and due dates, then perform retesting and issue closure evidence.
